title: Go SDK: streaming schema factory, retry annotations, and ergonomic typed decoders
preview:
Problem:
Teams building streaming LLM flows want typed, ergonomic decoding of structured outputs (JSON objects, jsonlines, and framed events) with clear semantics around retries. Current Go SDK helpers assume a single 'line-delimited JSON' framing and don't expose per-chunk retry safety signals. We need a model-agnostic factory for stream decoders, a decoder registry keyed by model+route/schema, and a small retry-annotation protocol that server responses can include to indicate whether a chunk is retry-safe.

Goals:
- Provide a StreamDecoderFactory that returns Decoder[T] for any typed struct T to keep strong typing in app code.
- Support multiple framings: json-lines, ndjson, framed-events ("event:" prefix), and envelope-with-retry-hint.
- Add a lightweight RetryHint concept in the response envelope: server may set retry_hint=partial|safe|none per-chunk. Client SDK uses this to determine if a backfilled request can be attempted without corruption.
- Keep public API ergonomic: minimal ceremony to get a typed streaming iterator that returns (T, meta) or an error.
- Ensure backwards compatibility: existing StreamDecoder helpers remain, but marked for deprecation once migration completes.

Scope (what this ticket does):
- Implement StreamDecoderFactory and Decoder[T] interface in go-sdk v0.27 runtime package.
- Implement decoder registry and a small DSL to register decoders for model+route combos.
- Add envelope parsing that extracts RetryHint and exposes it to retry middleware.
- Add unit tests, integration tests against the mock streaming server, and benchmark comparing throughput and allocations vs current helper.

Out of scope:
- Server-side changes beyond supporting the retry_hint field in our test harness (server rollout tracked in ENG-62001).
- Language SDKs other than Go (Python/TS follow-ups planned).

title: Priority wavefront admission and attention tiling
preview: Objective: Reduce 99th and 99.9th percentile end-to-end latency for mixed prefill+decode workloads by combining a priority 'wavefront' admission policy with an attention-tiling prefetch strategy and opportunistic kernel probing. Motivation: Current continuous batching handles average throughput well but exhibits long tails for heterogeneous request shapes and bursts that intermix long prefill requests with short chat turns. We need a low-risk runtime change that (1) improves tail latency without sacrificing throughput, (2) plays nicely with kernel selection heuristics, and (3) is rollable via canary. Key idea: when admitting prefill requests into a batch, tag each request with a wavefront priority derived from deadline, model shape, and observed per-tenant tail-profile. Admission then proceeds in a multi-pass wavefront: (a) fast-path wave for short/interactive requests (high priority), (b) standard coalesce wave for throughput, and (c) deferred wave for long prefill-heavy requests that can be split or serviced on a slower kernel path. Attention tiling: introduce a lightweight prefetcher that computes attention tile hints during prefill and stores compact attention-shard metadata in the KV cache header. During decode, kernel selection consults tile hints to choose tiled attention kernels when beneficial (lower memory pressure, better warp utilization). Kernel probing: add an opportunistic kernel probe step at admission to sample latency predictions across a short window (20-50ms) and attach a preferred-kernel hint to the request. We expect this to reduce kernel mis-selection for heterogenous shapes. Tradeoffs: extra admission CPU cycles and bookkeeping vs tail reduction; potential increased memory churn from tile metadata. Implementation plan: 1) spec data structures and proto for wavefront priority and tile hint (low-level binary header, backward-compatible). 2) implement admission wavefront scheduler with configurable thresholds and fallbacks. 3) attention-tiling prefetch worker that runs during prefill and annotates KV entries. 4) kernel probe runner that uses lightweight microbenchmarks on sampled batches. 5) integration: admission consults kernel hints + tiling hints and annotates request for the decode scheduler. 6) experiments and calibration with synthetic mix and production sampling. Success metrics (target): - p99 end-to-end latency reduction of 20% for mixed workloads - p99.9 reduction of 25% - <5% throughput regression for 95% of profiles - CPU admission overhead <6% of request CPU budget - No regression on single-tenant large-batch throughput Rollout: feature gating via config flag and runtime-1.25 canary pipeline. Backout path: revert flag; admission defaults to current coalescer. Monitoring: dashboard for per-tenant p50/p95/p99/p99.9, kernel-mismatch-rate, tile-hit-rate, admission-cpu-ops, and memorialized logs for deferred wave items.

title: Continuous Profile Optimizer + Batch Guard
preview:
Problem: teams run with static batching settings that degrade cost or latency as traffic shapes change. We need a continuous, low-risk system that recommends batching and cache parameters per workload and can apply them safely with automatic rollback if regressions are detected.

This ticket scopes the first iteration: a service ('profile-optimizer') that synthesizes workload profiles from request telemetry, derives heuristic parameter candidates (batch_size_target, max_latency_budget_ms, kv_cache_prefix_ttl, coalescing_window_ms), validates candidates in a safety sandbox, and exposes a staged apply path (recommend -> canary -> apply) with rollback triggers based on production SLIs/QLIs.

title: Ledger anchoring for progressive streaming credits and adaptive throttles
preview:
Problem: Streaming sessions that emit partial fragments (progressive outputs) are causing inconsistent token ledgers when combined with mid-stream model switches, dynamic throttles, and client reconnects. In several customer incidents we observed: (1) duplicate crediting when partial fragments are re-sent after reconnect, (2) under-crediting when preempted fragments are dropped by fallback routing, and (3) misattribution between input (prompt) and generated output tokens across multipart/noncanonical frames. This ticket implements a ledger anchoring mechanism plus an adaptive crediting protocol to make progressive streaming metering deterministic and robust to reconnects, backpressure, and model handoffs.

Scope: implement server-side ledger anchors per-session with idempotent fragment attribution; integrate anchors with the rate-limiter to ensure provisional credits can be rolled forward or rolled back deterministically; add a reconciliation job (fast-path) that runs on reconnect to compact provisional anchors and finalize credits. Changes span: streaming ingress middleware, metering pipeline, rate-limiter interaction, and billing finalizer service.

Goals: produce deterministic token tallies even under out-of-order frames, transient reconnects, and mid-session model variant switches; keep tail latency and throughput within current SLOs; provide transparent audit traces for customer support and billing disputes.

title: Cross-Product Launch Calendar: Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator
preview:
Objective: Build a Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO) that provides a single authoritative launch calendar and automated conflict-resolution layer for runtime, console, and API releases. DRTO should allow teams to reserve launch slots, attach canary windows, declare blackout windows and capacity reservations, and automatically compute safe stagger policies and fallback lanes when conflicts are detected.

Background: Launch friction has been a recurring source of latency and outages when multiple product teams schedule large runtime or console changes in overlapping windows. The current playbooks are manual (spreadsheets + Slack) and cause avoidable collisions, overbooked GPU capacity, and late-stage rollbacks. This initiative aims to reduce schedule conflicts, accelerate cross-team coordination, and provide automated signals for go/no-go decisions.

Scope (MVP):
- Central calendar service integrated into Redwood Console with read/write reservations for runtime, console, API releases.
- Basic conflict detection and advisory recommendations (suggest alternative slots, show capacity impact).
- Canary/cohort tagging and automatic canary window insertion for model-impacting runtime changes.
- Exportable cutover checklist generator and notification hooks for oncall and stakeholders.
- Integrations: GitHub PR metadata parsing (release tag), Jira/Linear link attachment, and a lightweight API to query capacity reservations.

Out of scope (MVP):
- Enforced scheduling locks that block deployments (will be considered after advisory pilot).
- Deep autoscaling automation tied to DRTO decisions (future).

Key stakeholders:
- Product: Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Runtime Engineering: Elena Park (lead)
- Console UX: Jonah Rivera (design lead)
- Platform Reliability: Samir Gupta (SRE review)
- Growth/Customer Ops: Hannah Lee (customer comms)

Design decisions and trade-offs:
- Advisory vs Enforced mode: MVP will be advisory only to avoid blocking teams during early rollout; enforcement can be added if advisory outcomes are proven reliable.
- Centralized calendar: pros = single source of truth; cons = potential bottleneck and increased coordination overhead. We'll mitigate with well-defined owner roles and delegated sub-calendars for large orgs.
- Automated slot recommendations will favor minimal model-impact windows for runtime upgrades (nighttime in region, low-traffic cohorts) but allow overrides with documented risk acceptance.

Metrics / Success criteria:
- Reduce schedule collisions (two+ major changes in overlapping capacity-sensitive windows) by 80% in first quarter after pilot.
- Decrease late-stage rollbacks attributable to scheduling conflicts by 50%.
- Mean time to resolve calendar conflicts (manual overrides reduced) under 24 hours.
- Adoption: 60% of runtime/console release owners use DRTO advisory for new releases within 3 months of pilot.

Operational plan and rollout:
1) Week 0-4: Implement calendar backend, reservation schema, and conflict detection engine.
2) Week 5-8: Console UI integration (read/write) + GitHub/Linear linking and notifications.
3) Week 9-10: Pilot with runtime team, advisory mode only, collect feedback and conflict metrics.
4) Week 11-14: Expand to console and API teams, add canary scheduling templates and cutover checklist exports.
5) Week 15+: Evaluate enforcement gates and capacity hedging automation for Dedicated customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reservation API exists and can be consumed by runtime pipelines.
- Console shows calendar with reservations, conflict markers, and suggestion panel.
- At least one pilot release uses DRTO advisory and logs telemetry to demonstrate conflict detection.
- Documentation and runbook for calendar owners and release stewards are published.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams ignore advisory recommendations leading to continued collisions. Mitigation: require visible risk acknowledgments on overrides and periodic governance reviews.
- Risk: Calendar becomes source of friction slowing launches. Mitigation: keep advisory only for MVP; invest in low-friction UI flows and API hooks.
- Risk: Capacity data freshness causes bad recommendations. Mitigation: ensure capacity syncs with Dedicated scheduler and add freshness indicators.

Artifacts and links:
- PRD: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DRTO-PRD (internal)
- Design spec (console flows): https://drive.redwood/drive/design/drto-v1
- Pilot schedule spreadsheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/DRTO-pilot
- Sample GitHub PR integration prototype: https://github.com/redwood-inference/drto-proto/pull/23
- Incident learnings relevant to scheduling collisions: https://confluence.redwood/incident/scheduling-collisions-2025

Next steps (short-term):
- Align pilot release owners and confirm pilot dates (action: Elena Park).
- UX mockups for reservation flow and conflict modal (action: Jonah Rivera).
- Runtime pipeline team to prototype reservation API client (action: Runtime Eng).

title: Strategic microcommit bands and rolling buffer allocation for feature waves
preview: Problem: As we scale Redwood Dedicated and Private onboarding, individual initiatives spike runway demand mid-cycle causing repeated mid-cycle scope cuts and last-minute reassignments. This ticket defines a lightweight, operational mechanism: 'microcommit bands' (small reserved effort bands per initiative) plus a rolling buffer allocation that moves between initiatives based on objective signals. Objective: reduce emergency scope cuts, increase predictability of cross-team staffing, and preserve high-priority launches without over-committing engineering capacity. Scope: define band sizing heuristic, buffer governance, automation hooks for signal ingestion (usage, blocker count, deploy risk), rollout plan for next three cycles, and dashboards to track buffer depletion and rebalancing activity.

title: Customer Advocate Handshake and Release Priority Protocol
preview:
Problem: Major enterprise customers and strategic partners currently have ad-hoc escalation paths into the roadmap. That variability causes misaligned expectations, late-stage scope creep, and unclear commitments for both Product and Engineering. We lack a repeatable 'handshake' that turns a high-touch customer ask into a scoped, measurable roadmap commitment with clear fallbacks and billing/contract triggers.

Goal: Define and operationalize a lightweight but enforceable Customer Advocate Handshake (CAH) that: 1) standardizes intake for top accounts, 2) produces a scoped Linear ticket with acceptance criteria and rollout plan, 3) surfaces necessary legal/finance/ops triggers, and 4) maps the result to OKRs and release slots with clear canaries/fallbacks.

Proposed approach:
- Intake form filled by Customer Advocate (CA) including business impact, customer urgency, usage signals, expected ROI, and acceptance criteria.
- 5-step handshake: (1) CA submits intake, (2) PM triages & scores (decision lattice), (3) Technical review with Eng + Infra (risk/cost), (4) Contract/Finance/Legal gating if revenue or SLA changes, (5) Commitment doc + release slot + canary plan.
- Scoring lattice: revenue impact, churn risk, technical effort, strategic alignment, and operational risk. Weighting is editable per quarter.
- Fallback policy: timeboxed partial-deliverables or feature flags; automatic downgrade to maintenance priority after commit window if billing/contract not completed.

Why this matters: Reduces time-to-commit, sets consistent expectations for customers and internal stakeholders, and creates an auditable trail of decisions tied to OKRs (e.g., OKR-Product 2026.Q1: Reduce escalated intake cycle time by 50%).

title: Error Budget Federation and Cross-Team Commitment Matrix
preview:
Goal: Define and implement a federated error-budget model plus a commitment matrix so teams have predictable runway for feature rollouts while preserving global SLOs.

Problem: Currently error budgets are tracked only at a product-wide level. Team leads lack clear, actionable allocations and lightweight APIs to reserve tranches for experiments or rollouts. This leads to ad-hoc rollouts, surprise SLO burns, and emergency rollbacks.

Scope:
- Design allocation rules: baseline reservation for infra/critical services, per-product tranches, and experiment buckets.
- Implement a Commitment Matrix (spreadsheet + API) that maps teams -> weekly tranche -> guardrails.
- Extend SLO dashboard with per-team rollup and tranche-level burn charts.
- Integrate gating into Redwood Console: canary/rollout policies check team tranche before increasing % traffic.
- Alerts & runbook: automated alerts to channel + playbook for reclaiming budget, pausing rollouts, or requesting temporary extension.

Design constraints/assumptions:
- Global SLOs remain the source of truth; federation cannot increase aggregate burn.
- Reserve 20% of monthly error budget for platform/infra critical incidents.
- Weekly tranches preferred over daily for lower noise.
- Teams may request extra tranche via an approval flow (PM + SRE).

Out-of-scope for this ticket: automatic repayment/redistribution algorithms (follow-up), per-customer budgets, and billing changes.

Why this matters: federating budgets reduces cross-team thrash, gives product owners confidence to run canaries, and produces measurable runway that informs roadmap prioritization.

title: Sprint Lab: Roadmap Threading & Scenario Lanes for Hosted API + Model Catalog
preview:
Objective: Create a compact, decision-ready Q2 roadmap for Hosted API + Model Catalog that balances reliability SLAs, cost optimization, and an experimental runway for new open models. We will run a "Scenario Lab" over two sprints to validate trade-offs across latency, unit cost, and quality so leadership can commit tranche dates and capacity hedges.

Why now: Usage growth and new open-model variants require a clearer phasing plan to avoid surprise capacity costs and quality regressions. Current workstreams (model onboarding, runtime kernel selection, and console rollout) are loosely aligned; this ticket unifies them into lane-based deliverables and explicit OKR ties.

High-level approach: split the roadmap into three scenario lanes: (1) Stability Lane — SLA-backed endpoints and conservative model rotations; (2) Cost Lane — quantized/packed variants and batching strategies for cost-sensitive customers; (3) Experiment Lane — canary-first model integrations and structured evals. For each lane we will: define tranche milestones, required engineering changes, console UX rollouts, and rollback/guardrail criteria.

Deliverables: tranche calendar with dates, OKR alignment doc, canary schedule + rollback playbook, capacity hedge (reserved vs burst) with cost estimate, Scenario Lab benchmarks (latency/cost/quality matrix), and a PRD + stakeholder sign-off.

Success metrics: leadership-approved tranche calendar; clear per-lane cost delta estimates (target: cost lane reduces per-token cost by >=20% for selected workloads); P99 latency SLOs defined for Stability Lane; experiment lane has pass/fail regression thresholds on eval harness.

Risks & trade-offs: prioritizing cost lane could increase regression risk; experiments increase operational overhead and require robust rollback. We will mitigate with automated regression gates tied to the eval harness and limit experiment blast radius via region-limited canaries.

For each lane we will: define tranche milestones, required engineering changes, console UX rollouts, and rollback/guardrail criteria.

Deliverables: tranche calendar with dates, OKR alignment doc, canary schedule + rollback playbook, capacity hedge (reserved vs burst) with cost estimate, Scenario Lab benchmarks (latency/cost/quality matrix), and a PRD + stakeholder sign-off.

Success metrics: leadership-approved tranche calendar; clear per-lane cost delta estimates (target: cost lane reduces per-token cost by >=20% for selected workloads); P99 latency SLOs defined for Stability Lane; experiment lane has pass/fail regression thresholds on eval harness.

Risks & trade-offs: prioritizing cost lane could increase regression risk; experiments increase operational overhead and require robust rollback. We will mitigate with automated regression gates tied to the eval harness and limit experiment blast radius via region-limited canaries.

title: Sprint orchestra and fallback-lane runbook for heterogeneous runtime rollouts
preview:
Overview:
This ticket captures a runbook, ownership matrix, and sprint-level cadence for rolling out runtime changes (kernel swaps, batching logic, and quantization toggles) across heterogeneous fleet segments (hosted, dedicated, private). Goal is predictable latency SLOs while enabling fast roll-forward for critical fixes.

Scope & Acceptance Criteria:
- Define orchestration lanes (canary, regional wave, enterprise-only) with clear capacity hedges and warmup windows.
- Document fallback lanes and automated routing (policy) to downgrade to compatible model variants or older runtime build.
- Provide a checklist for preflight verifications: perf bench (p50/p95), KV cache hit validation, tracing hooks.
- Create post-deploy telemetry dashboard and regression alert thresholds.

Progress updates:
- 2026-02-22: Draft runbook created (Confluence: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/RRO/pages/Runtime+Sprint+Orchestra).
- 2026-02-28: Spike completed against canary cluster; telemetry shows +12% p95 in worst-case synthetic traffic. Artifact: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1RRunbookArtifacts (perf CSVs).
- 2026-03-03: Eng review (Diego, Hanna) recommended adding a capacity-hedge lane for Dedicated customers to reserve burst pool. Action: add autoscale policy checklist.
- 2026-03-07: Design feedback from Lena: UI needs a new "rollout safety" flag in Console to surface active hedges and auto-fallback triggers (linked PRD: https://docs.google.com/document/d/PRD-rollout-safety).
- 2026-03-10: Canaries extended to regional waves after adjusting batching config; observed KV cache warmup reduced p95 delta to +4%. Telemetry dashboard: https://app.datadog.com/dash/12345/runtime-rollout (internal).

Decisions & Trade-offs:
- Trade-off accepted: prefer conservative p95 targets during rolling windows to avoid broad customer impact; this increases rollout time by ~30% but reduces rollback frequency.
- Fallback mechanism: implement route-level policy that prefers model-variant fallback over full rollback when compatibility permits (lower user-visible disruption).
- Autoscale vs reserved capacity: for enterprise lanes use reserved burst pool; for hosted tier prefer autoscale to optimize cost.

Risks & Mitigations:
- Risk: abrupt KV cache miss storms after kernel change. Mitigation: staggered warmup + smoke tests that exercise long-context flows.
- Risk: operator confusion during multi-lane roll. Mitigation: ownership matrix (on-call + rollout lead) and a one-click rollback documented in runbook.

Test Plan:
- Preflight: perf bench (p50/p95), end-to-end smoke across three regions, canary traffic replay (10% of production), degradation simulation using traffic-shaper.
- Post-deploy: 30-minute verification window, followed by 6-hour monitoring with regression alerts on #rollouts channel.

- Quantify burst pool sizing for Dedicated customers (owner: ENG capacity team; dependency ENG-2412).
- Finalize Console surface for the "rollout safety" flag (owner: Design; due 2026-03-20).

title: probe failure-mode: prevent cascading capacity churn during region failover
preview:
Problem: During a recent multi-region failover exercise we observed cascading capacity churn and transient 5xxs as readiness probes oscillated for pods coming up in the target region. Rapid pod restarts and immediate autoscaler triggers amplified the problem: probes flapped, the HPA scaled up aggressively, new pods hit cold KV cache misses, latency SLOs breached, and circuit breakers opened downstream.

Goal: Implement a set of defensive changes to probe behavior, autoscaler stabilization, and load-shedding coordination so that region failovers and partial network/dep degradations do not cause secondary outages or SLO regressions. We want measurable improvements in availability and reduced variability during failover while keeping recovery time acceptable.

title: operator fencing caused cascading tenant reconcile delays; staged hotfix and staggered redeploy plan
preview: Summary: Multiple on-prem customers reported prolonged tenant upgrade stalls after control-plane operator fencing events. Symptoms: operator enters short-lived fence state, leader election flips rapidly, tenant controllers delay reconciles for 10+ minutes leading to customer application errors and upgrade timeouts. Impacted modes: Private (VPC) and on-prem deployments using helm-managed operator. This ticket tracks triage, an emergency hotfix branch, rollout plan, and post-deploy verification.

title: Staged ledger consolidation with probabilistic idempotency and windowed settlement
preview:
Problem: We currently observe intermittent divergence between real-time meter tallies and final invoice-ready ledgers when late events (retries, region duplicates) arrive after the settlement window. Root causes include non-deterministic batching, out-of-order partition delivery, and opportunistic dedupe heuristics that are conservative and sometimes discard valid retroactive corrections.

Goal: Build a staged consolidation pipeline that performs windowed settlement with an explicit probabilistic idempotency scoring pass followed by a deterministic commit phase. The pipeline should: (1) accept late-arriving events with bounded correction windows, (2) provide deterministic final ledgers for billing, (3) support shadow-run canaries for safe rollout, and (4) surface lineage for audit and support.

High-level design:
- Ingest: events enter via existing usage-stream (Kafka topic usage.v2). An enrichment layer attaches route, model, tenant, request_id, and an arrival_timestamp_ms.
- Stage 1 (Resolve): short-term aggregation into 1s micro-batches for low-latency metering (used by live console counters). A lightweight idempotency filter drops exact dupes but keeps maximal data for Stage 2.
- Stage 2 (Score): time-windowed shuffles (configurable, default 5m) compute a probabilistic idempotency score using a bloom-based fingerprint and request-level heuristics (kv-cache hit, model-seed). Scores are used to tag events as: new|probable-dup|likely-correction.
- Stage 3 (Consolidate): for each tenant-route-window we compute a provisional settlement delta using weighted aggregation (probable-dup given weight <1). Provisional deltas are emitted to a shadow ledger and to observability streams.
- Stage 4 (Commit): after the correction window closes (configurable per-tenant, default 2h), a deterministic re-evaluation merges all events for the window and performs a final, idempotent commit to the canonical billing ledger (Cassandra-backed with idempotent primary keys).
- Late-arrival compensation: events that arrive after Commit are recorded to a corrections table and surfaced for manual or automated adjustments, with TTL and rate-limited backfill jobs.

Operational requirements and SLOs:
- Correctness: final ledger must match reconciled source of truth with <0.05% divergence for >95% of tenants over a 7-day window.
- Latency: provisional tallies in Stage 1 must be available to Console within 5s for 99th percentile.
- Cost: per-token processing overhead should not exceed 15% over current baseline in steady-state.
- Observability: lineage trace per settled delta, + per-tenant reconciliation dashboard and drift alerts.

Trade-offs / rationale:
- Probabilistic scoring reduces false positives for dedupe at the cost of producing provisional (not final) values; it enables earlier visibility for product while preserving correctness in the commit phase.
- Deterministic commit uses a heavier compute pass but runs less frequently and only on bounded windows to limit cost.

Security / compliance notes: all event enrichment and fingerprints must be computed using tenant-scoped salts (KMS-managed) to avoid cross-tenant correlation. This change requires a security review (see security_review_required).

Rollout plan: shadow-run for 72h comparing provisional deltas to existing reconciler, then a canary commit on 3 low-volume tenants, then incremental ramp with manual approvals. Final cutover after validating zero drift on canaries for 48h.

Links: Confluence design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/BILL/Staged+Ledger+Consolidation (internal). RFC: https://docs.redwood.ai/rfc/staged-ledger-consolidation.

title: Quantization profiles: mixed‑precision throughput vs quality contour benchmarking
preview:
Goal: Build a benchmark and load harness that maps throughput (tokens/sec) and p95 latency to quality for a range of quantization profiles (FP16, INT8, INT4, and mixed-precision fallbacks). This ticket covers spec, harness implementation, quality metric plumbing, regression detection rules, and dashboards to visualize quality/perf contours and automated alerts when a quant profile crosses a regression threshold.

Scope and constraints:
- Run mixed-precision permutations where compute kernels use FP16 for attention and INT8/INT4 for MLP where supported. Include explicit fallback variants (e.g., INT4->INT8->FP16) and measure cost/latency tradeoffs.
- Collect tokens/sec, p50/p95/p99 latency, memory footprint, kernel utilization, and end-to-end quality metrics (token-level perplexity proxy + embedding cosine similarity and ROUGE on a canonical prompt set).
- Support traffic modes: synthetic steady-state, production-trace replay (sampled requests), and bursty arrival patterns.
- Include KV-cache warmup experiments and cold-cache baselines.
- Provide an automated regression detector using EWMA+CUSUM for latency and a separate quality drift detector (paired permutation t-test on similarity distributions), with configurable SLOs.
- Output consumable artifacts: CSV/Parquet run exports, a contour heatmap generator, and Grafana dashboards with example alerts.

Non-goals:
- Model re-training or calibration. Policy or rollout automation beyond reporting.

title: Cross‑lang ops starter pack: pragmatic streaming & retries templates
preview:
Goal: Ship a compact, opinionated starter pack (repo + docs) that shows pragmatic patterns for streaming ergonomics, deterministic retries, and small server/CLI examples across TypeScript (Next.js), Python (FastAPI), and Go (HTTP server + CLI).

Why: We keep getting support tickets and onboarding questions around how to wire streaming responses into modern frontends and servers while handling retries, cancellation, and partial result resumption. This ticket bundles lightweight, production-minded samples and cookbook snippets teams can copy into their apps.

Deliverables:
- nextjs-edge-streaming: Minimal Next.js app showing client-side streaming consumption (fetch with ReadableStream + AbortController), token-level progress UI, and a server-edge route that proxies Redwood streaming responses with correct headers.
- fastapi-async-stream: FastAPI sample demonstrating an async generator for chunked responses, graceful shutdown hooks, background tasks for long-running decoding, and example middleware to translate retry semantics into idempotent server handlers.
- go-http-resume: Go 1.20+ HTTP server showing context-aware request handling, streaming via chunked Transfer-Encoding, ordered chunk IDs for resumable streams, and a small CLI to demonstrate resumable downloads.
- cli-idempotent: Cross-lang CLI patterns (Python/Go/TS) showing safe retry wrappers, exponential backoff with jitter, and how to persist minimal retry state (cursor/token) to resume interrupted streams.
- cookbook-snippets.md: Short recipes for batching, prefix caching, backoff tuning, and instrumentation hooks (metrics/timing).

Non-goals: Full reference SDK integration tests, enterprise deployment scripts. Keep samples 1–2 files per language and readable in < 10 minutes.

Implementation notes:
- Prefer standard web streaming (ReadableStream / fetch) for Next.js edge sample — avoids server-sent events complexity and works in edge runtimes.
- For FastAPI use async generators and streaming response; keep dependency list minimal (httpx for examples).
- For Go, favor net/http + context cancellation over heavy frameworks; include example using chi only if requested in review.
- Retry semantics: show client-side idempotency keys and server-friendly resumable cursors. Avoid inventing protocol; demonstrate patterns that map to Redwood routing/caching (e.g., using model variant fallback headers).

preview: Goal: Ship a defensible, safe-to-run automated remediator plus an on-call runbook that addresses the most frequent Private (VPC) healthcheck failures. This ticket covers: (1) canonical detection thresholds and alert mappings; (2) an automation state machine that performs idempotent corrective actions; (3) tight Terraform/IaC repair hooks to fix drift without risking destructive changes; (4) canary and rollout gating for upgrades that touch private nodes; and (5) clear rollback and escalation paths for oncall.

title: Sinuous regional routing policy: composite latency + capacity steering with safe fallbacks
preview: Design and implement a new multi-region request steering policy (codename: 'sinuous') that combines latency-based routing, geo pinning for affinity, and capacity-aware steering into a single compact decision function. The goal is to improve SLO adherence for 99th pct latency during cross-region incidents while minimizing unnecessary cross-region egress and avoiding capacity hot-spots. The policy must integrate circuit breaker signals and graded load-shedding tiers for graceful degradation.

title: Transient Limiter Outage Recovery Agent - SLO-preserving soft-admission and cross-region reconciliation
preview: Background: Recent incidents show that transient outages of the global limiter control plane can cause either hard denials (customers blocked) or uncontrolled open-admit behavior during failover windows, both of which cause SLO violations. Problem statement: we need a mechanism to bridge short-lived limiter outages without violating latency and error SLOs and while preserving per-tenant fairness and billing correctness. Goal: design and implement a locally-run Recovery Agent that proxies admission decisions when the primary quota store is unavailable. The Agent must be SLO-aware (maintain p50/p95/p99 latency budgets), tenant-aware (avoid starvation), and reconciliatory (merge local state back to global quota store after recovery). Core idea: a soft-admission proxy with three coupled capabilities: 1) Latency-aware admission: per-tenant residual latency budget tokens determine how aggressively we admit requests; Agent tracks observed latencies and reduces admission probability for tenants nearing their latency budgets. 2) Probabilistic credit aging: instead of fixed bursts, local credits age with a decay curve and admission is sampled probabilistically to avoid synchronized spikes. 3) Cross-region reconciliation: after limiter recovery, local ledgers exchange digestable deltas and reconcile via idempotent operations and tombstone markers to avoid double-counting. Important safety features: circuit breaker thresholds, global kill-switch (operator-controlled), and conservative default admission that favors preserving latency over raw throughput. This ticket scopes the design, prototype, and initial canary on one region, with telemetry and failback automation.

title: Topology-aware KV-tiling continuous batcher for mixed-length streams
preview:
Background: Continuous batching performs well for homogeneous sequence lengths but we see persistent GPU memory fragmentation and tail latency increases when request mixes contain many short prompts interleaved with long responses. Current KV residency and slab allocation are conservative and cause underutilized GPU memory and frequent kv-evictions during long-turn spikes.

Scope: Implement a topology-aware KV-tiling policy in the continuous batcher that (a) partitions KV residency into per-cohort tiles by length/turn profile, (b) uses a lightweight reservoir admission controller to pin tiles temporarily during long-response phases, and (c) coordinates with the slot allocator to bias packing so that mixed-length cohorts co-reside in a cache-friendly layout. The change must preserve latency SLOs for P0 routes and reduce average token cost by lowering unnecessary evictions and GPU slab churn.

Non-goals: This ticket does not refactor the kernel-selection layer or change quantization heuristics. It also does not modify the API surface or customer-facing behavior beyond performance improvements.

Constraints: Must operate within the existing continuous-batcher runtime with minimal changes to the fast path (<=1 additional branch on hot path). Must be rollback-safe with a feature flag and have telemetry for tile occupancy, eviction rates, and failed pin attempts.

title: Release-train comms, ops runbook, and release-notes digest for API/Console/Runtime
preview:
Summary: Coordinate a single, consolidated release-train communications and operational runbook that covers milestones, scope-lock signals, cut criteria, release-notes generation, and GTM tasks across API, Console, and Runtime teams. Goal is to reduce ambiguity about last-minute scope decisions, automate a digest for release notes, and codify a lightweight hypercare plan for the first 72 hours post-rollout.

Background: Recent rollouts had fragmented comms between runtime and console teams resulting in mismatched docs, late bugfix rollouts, and duplicated rollback efforts. This initiative centralizes cutline decisions and creates an automated release-notes digest that compiles: PRs merged after freeze, critical known issues, upgrade notes, and per-team owner blurbs.

Scope:
- Milestone calendar and signoff checklist for MAJOR, MINOR, and PATCH release lanes.
- Cut criteria matrix (functional, perf, security, docs) and who has veto authority.
- Release-notes digest template and automation spec (GitHub action + Confluence integration).
- Communication matrix with channel templates (Slack, email, release board) and stakeholders.
- 72-hour hypercare plan including on-call rotation, SLA escalations, and rollback playbook.
- Postmortem and evaluation plan for quality/regression metrics tied to the release.

Out of scope: Full localization of release notes (will be addressed as a follow-up), long-form marketing collateral, and marketplace listings updates.

title: Dedicated: per-tenant/cluster batching tier overrides + precedence rules
preview:
Dedicated customers need deterministic control of batching tier/profile selection with clear override precedence.

Implement:
- Per-tenant override: ability for Dedicated tenants to force a batching tier (fast|standard|cost_efficient) and/or explicit batching knob overrides (where allowed) at the tenant/cluster level.
- Cluster override: ability for Redwood ops to pin a cluster-level default tier for a Dedicated cluster when capacity characteristics differ (GPU type mix, MIG, etc.).
- Precedence definition (must be documented and enforced consistently across control plane + runtime):
  1) Customer override (tenant/cluster level in Dedicated) 
  2) Route policy tier selection (route/model policy configuration)
  3) Tier default selected by workload-aware logic (runtime heuristics)

Non-goals (for this ticket):
- Per-request overrides for Dedicated (we may add later; risk of abuse/high cardinality telemetry).
- Auto-tuning per tenant.

Links:
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/prd-batching-profiles-slo-tiers.json
- Design doc: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/workload-aware-batching-defaults-design.json
- API surface notes: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/tier-config-api-proposal-notes.json

Implementation notes / constraints:
- Must be backwards compatible with existing Dedicated configs that set manual batching knobs.
- Must be explainable: reason codes should reflect the final source of truth (override vs route policy vs tier default vs overload mode).
- Must not introduce high-cardinality metric blowups (e.g., arbitrary override IDs as labels).

Acceptance criteria:
- Control plane config supports setting tenant-level and cluster-level batching tier overrides with validation.
- Runtime receives a resolved effective tier + allowed knob overrides (if applicable) without ambiguous merging.
- Precedence is enforced and unit-tested:
  - customer override > route policy > tier default
  - if customer override selects an incompatible tier/profile for a model/hardware constraint, runtime falls back deterministically (documented) and emits reason code.
- Console/debug output (at least internal) can show: effective tier, source, and reason code.
- Docs snippet for Dedicated ops/support describing precedence and troubleshooting steps.

Test plan:
- Unit tests for precedence evaluation (policy engine) + snapshot tests for resolved config.
- Integration test in staging Dedicated cluster: set tenant override, verify it supersedes route policy, verify reason code.
- Regression test: existing routes with no explicit tier continue to use workload-aware tier defaults.

Rollout:
- Behind feature flag for Dedicated only (paired with ENG-18431 kill switch).
- Start with 1 internal Dedicated tenant + 1 canary tenant (opt-in) before general availability.

Owner coordination:
- Product requirements: Lauren Bishop (Dedicated)
- Policy/config implementation: Ethan Park + Benji Okafor + Jordan Lee
- Runtime enforcement hooks: Sofia Ivanova / Lena Fischer (consult)
- Observability: Allison Grant (consult for reason code + label guidance)

title: Implement tiered batching profile config schema + validation (incl. migration)
preview:
Goal
Ship a backwards-compatible config schema for three batching SLO tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient), plus validation rules and a migration plan for existing manual batching configs.

Context
We’re introducing tiered defaults that map to scheduler knobs. The runtime needs a clear, stable configuration contract that:
- works across Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private deployments
- supports route-level policy selection and per-tenant overrides (Dedicated)
- prevents foot-guns (conflicting knobs, impossible combinations, accidental global tier changes)
- supports safe rollout (feature flag, kill switch, and easy rollback)

Scope (this ticket)
1) Define the config schema (control plane + runtime consumption)
2) Implement validation + precedence rules
3) Provide migration behavior for existing configs
4) Add docs/notes for downstream integrators (Console + SDKs) on the schema contract

Non-goals
- Choosing actual knob values per tier (handled in design/benchmarking work)
- Per-tenant real-time auto-tuning (out of scope for v1)

Proposed schema (high level)
We need two orthogonal concepts:
A) Tier selection policy (what tier is desired for this route/request)
B) Tier profiles (what scheduler knob set corresponds to each tier)

A) Tier selection policy
- Default: standard
- Selection sources (in descending precedence):
  1. Dedicated tenant override (cluster/tenant scoped)
  2. Route policy (model/route config)
  3. Request hint (header/SDK param) if allowed by policy
  4. Global default
- Guardrails: allow request-level hint only if route policy explicitly enables it (avoid customers accidentally “spraying” fast tier across all traffic).

B) Tier profiles
- Profiles are expressed as a small, stable set of scheduler knobs (don’t expose every internal knob).
- Profiles can be:
  - “built-in / managed” (default) → runtime ships known profiles keyed by (model family, hardware class)
  - “custom override” (enterprise/Dedicated) → explicit knob overrides gated by allowlist + validation

Concrete fields to support (draft)
- batching.tier: fast | standard | cost_efficient
- batching.allow_request_tier_override: true/false
- batching.profile_source: managed | custom
- batching.custom_profile: (set of knobs; only permitted when profile_source=custom)
- batching.overload_mode: (policy enum; e.g., shed | clamp_batching | degrade_to_standard; final naming TBD but validation must allow only known values)

NOTE: The runtime can keep internal knobs private; the control plane schema should focus on a “safe knob surface” (max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, prefill_max_batch_tokens, decode_max_batch_tokens, admission_control parameters, etc.). We’ll finalize the exact knob set with Sofia/Lena; validation should be written to allow additions without breaking older configs.

Validation rules (must-haves)
- Mutually exclusive: tier selection vs custom knobs
  - If batching.profile_source=managed → custom_profile must be empty
  - If batching.profile_source=custom → tier must still be set (for telemetry + dashboards), but managed profile lookup is bypassed
- Request override safety
  - batching.allow_request_tier_override=false → ignore request hint + emit reason code “REQUEST_HINT_IGNORED_POLICY”
- Enum + bounds validation
  - Reject unknown tier values
  - Bounds-check custom knobs (e.g., max_wait_ms within [0, 20] for fast, [0, 100] for standard/cost-efficient unless explicitly allowlisted)
  - Reject configs that can cause starvation (e.g., decode_max_batch_tokens=0 or contradictory prefill/decode caps)
- Back-compat
  - Existing configs with legacy knobs should still parse
  - When legacy knobs are present, runtime should:
    - prefer explicit legacy knobs over managed tier defaults (for safety)
    - emit a migration warning metric/log: “LEGACY_BATCHING_KNOBS_PRESENT”

Migration plan
Phase 0 (now): Introduce schema that can represent tiers without changing behavior.
- Parse tier fields, but behind feature flag default to “no-op” (standard behavior)
- Emit structured warnings when both legacy knobs and new tier fields are set

Phase 1 (hosted canary): Enable tiered defaults only when:
- no legacy knobs are present, AND
- the feature flag is enabled for the route/tenant

Phase 2 (GA):
- Keep legacy fields supported for N releases (proposal: 2 minor releases)
- Provide automated migration guidance in Console (follow-up) and docs: “remove legacy knobs to opt into tiered defaults.”

Open questions / decisions needed
- Exact list of safe custom knobs for Dedicated overrides (Lauren Bishop wants clear precedence and minimal surface area).
- Whether request-level tier hints are supported for Hosted API in v1 vs only via route policy (Henry Cho/Nikhil Sharma input).
- Whether we treat “cost-efficient” as “cost_efficient” in config to avoid hyphen issues; propose: external-facing is “cost-efficient”, config uses “cost_efficient”.

Implementation notes
- Control plane: extend config protobuf/JSON schema + validation at write-time.
- Runtime: add a small config adapter that resolves the effective tier + profile source, then passes a normalized structure into scheduler.
- Add structured “effective batching config” to debug endpoints (redacted/safe) to help Support.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Schema supports specifying tier at route policy level
- [x] Validation rejects invalid enums and conflicting settings
- [x] Legacy configs continue to work with no behavior change
- [x] Clear precedence rules are implemented and tested (unit + integration)
- [x] Metrics/logging for migration warnings and “effective tier/profile source” are emitted (reason codes implemented in ENG-18426, but this ticket must output the fields needed)
- [ ] Signed off by Dedicated PM on override semantics (Lauren Bishop)

Testing plan
- Unit tests for schema validation (invalid combinations, bounds)
- Golden tests for config resolution precedence
- Integration test with runtime: ensure effective tier defaults to standard when unset
- Backwards-compat tests with representative legacy configs from Hosted + Dedicated

Rollout
- Land behind feature flag (ENG-18431)
- Canary in one Hosted region + subset of routes (Nikhil Sharma)
- Dedicated opt-in follows after override precedence work (ENG-18430)

Risks
- Misconfiguration leading to silent tier changes; mitigate with: explicit feature flag + reason codes + migration warnings.
- Config drift across Hosted/Dedicated/Private; mitigate by: one canonical schema package + shared validation.

Owners / reviewers
- Owner: Ethan Park
- Core reviewers: Benji Okafor (rollout primitives), Jordan Lee (policy engine), Sofia Ivanova (scheduler needs), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Lauren Bishop (Dedicated requirements)

title: Harden marketplace entitlement sync job (retries, idempotency, alerting)
preview:
Context
Marketplace buyers (AWS + GCP) are hitting provisioning delays after subscribing because our entitlement sync is occasionally failing silently, retrying too slowly, or producing inconsistent results across repeated runs. This directly impacts the subscription → entitlement → org provisioning funnel and has been a recurring escalation path (see SUP-4821).

Goal
Improve reliability and debuggability of the entitlement sync job:
- Faster and safer recovery from transient partner/API issues (retries + backoff)
- Idempotency so repeated sync runs do not create duplicate entitlements / churn state
- Strong alerting and runbook to reduce time-to-detect/resolve

Scope
Applies to:
- Scheduled entitlement sync job (poll + reconcile)
- On-demand sync trigger used by console onboarding retry flows
- AWS Marketplace Entitlement / Metering integration and GCP Marketplace procurement entitlement lookup (as implemented in our entitlement service)

Out of scope
- Changes to listing copy / docs
- New SKU definitions (handled in billing catalog work)
- Long-term architectural changes (e.g., streaming/webhook-only entitlements). This is hardening + guardrails.

Problem statement (observed)
1) Transient upstream errors cause the job to give up and wait until the next scheduled run, leading to 15–60 minute delays.
2) Partial writes: if we successfully fetch partner entitlements but fail mid-write, the next run may reprocess and create duplicate rows or flip states.
3) We lack actionable alerts: we can see “customers complaining” before we see “job is failing.”
4) Entitlement propagation delays are normal on partner side; our UX currently encourages retries, but backend isn’t robust to repeated triggers.

Proposed changes
A) Retries + backoff
- Add bounded retries for upstream partner calls (AWS/GCP) on retryable errors (timeouts, 429, 5xx).
- Use exponential backoff with jitter; cap max delay per attempt.
- Separate retry policies for:
  - Partner API calls
  - Internal DB writes/transactions
  - Downstream side effects (provisioning webhook triggers)
- Ensure job runtime stays under scheduler limits; if a run is “too unhealthy,” fail fast and alert.

B) Idempotency + transactional safety
- Introduce an idempotency key per (provider, external_customer_id, external_subscription_id, entitlement_id/version) to dedupe repeated processing.
- Store last-seen partner snapshot hash (or partner revision token if available) per subscription; skip processing if unchanged.
- Perform write operations in a single transaction per subscription reconciliation:
  - Upsert entitlement records
  - Update subscription status
  - Enqueue provisioning updates (outbox pattern)
- Enforce uniqueness constraints (DB) to prevent duplicate entitlements even if code regresses.

C) Concurrency controls
- Ensure only one active reconciliation per (provider, subscription) at a time.
- Use advisory locks (or equivalent) around subscription reconciliation to avoid double-processing when scheduled run overlaps with an on-demand trigger.

D) Observability + alerting
Metrics (tagged by provider=aws|gcp, environment, error_class):
- entitlement_sync_run_count
- entitlement_sync_run_duration_ms (p50/p95)
- entitlement_sync_partner_request_count + partner_request_latency_ms
- entitlement_sync_retry_count
- entitlement_sync_reconciled_subscriptions_count
- entitlement_sync_failed_subscriptions_count
- entitlement_sync_outbox_lag_seconds
- entitlement_sync_last_success_timestamp (per provider)

Alerts (Pager + Slack):
- P0: No successful sync run in > 15 minutes (prod) OR error rate > 5% over 10 minutes
- P1: Outbox lag > 5 minutes sustained OR retries spiking (indicative of throttling)
- P2: Reconcile duration p95 > baseline * 2 for 30 minutes

Add structured logs

text:
Context
Marketplace buyers (AWS + GCP) are hitting provisioning delays after subscribing because our entitlement sync is occasionally failing silently, retrying too slowly, or producing inconsistent results across repeated runs. This directly impacts the subscription → entitlement → org provisioning funnel and has been a recurring escalation path (see SUP-4821).

Goal
Improve reliability and debuggability of the entitlement sync job:
- Faster and safer recovery from transient partner/API issues (retries + backoff)
- Idempotency so repeated sync runs do not create duplicate entitlements / churn state
- Strong alerting and runbook to reduce time-to-detect/resolve

Scope
Applies to:
- Scheduled entitlement sync job (poll + reconcile)
- On-demand sync trigger used by console onboarding retry flows
- AWS Marketplace Entitlement / Metering integration and GCP Marketplace procurement entitlement lookup (as implemented in our entitlement service)

Out of scope
- Changes to listing copy / docs
- New SKU definitions (handled in billing catalog work)
- Long-term architectural changes (e.g., streaming/webhook-only entitlements). This is hardening + guardrails.

Problem statement (observed)
1) Transient upstream errors cause the job to give up and wait until the next scheduled run, leading to 15–60 minute delays.
2) Partial writes: if we successfully fetch partner entitlements but fail mid-write, the next run may reprocess and create duplicate rows or flip states.
3) We lack actionable alerts: we can see “customers complaining” before we see “job is failing.”
4) Entitlement propagation delays are normal on partner side; our UX currently encourages retries, but backend isn’t robust to repeated triggers.

Proposed changes
A) Retries + backoff
- Add bounded retries for upstream partner calls (AWS/GCP) on retryable errors (timeouts, 429, 5xx).
- Use exponential backoff with jitter; cap max delay per attempt.
- Separate retry policies for:
  - Partner API calls
  - Internal DB writes/transactions
  - Downstream side effects (provisioning webhook triggers)
- Ensure job runtime stays under scheduler limits; if a run is “too unhealthy,” fail fast and alert.

B) Idempotency + transactional safety
- Introduce an idempotency key per (provider, external_customer_id, external_subscription_id, entitlement_id/version) to dedupe repeated processing.
- Store last-seen partner snapshot hash (or partner revision token if available) per subscription; skip processing if unchanged.
- Perform write operations in a single transaction per subscription reconciliation:
  - Upsert entitlement records
  - Update subscription status
  - Enqueue provisioning updates (outbox pattern)
- Enforce uniqueness constraints (DB) to prevent duplicate entitlements even if code regresses.

C) Concurrency controls
- Ensure only one active reconciliation per (provider, subscription) at a time.
- Use advisory locks (or equivalent) around subscription reconciliation to avoid double-processing when scheduled run overlaps with an on-demand trigger.

D) Observability + alerting
Metrics (tagged by provider=aws|gcp, environment, error_class):
- entitlement_sync_run_count
- entitlement_sync_run_duration_ms (p50/p95)
- entitlement_sync_partner_request_count + partner_request_latency_ms
- entitlement_sync_retry_count
- entitlement_sync_reconciled_subscriptions_count
- entitlement_sync_failed_subscriptions_count
- entitlement_sync_outbox_lag_seconds
- entitlement_sync_last_success_timestamp (per provider)

Alerts (Pager + Slack):
- P0: No successful sync run in > 15 minutes (prod) OR error rate > 5% over 10 minutes
- P1: Outbox lag > 5 minutes sustained OR retries spiking (indicative of throttling)
- P2: Reconcile duration p95 > baseline * 2 for 30 minutes

Add structured logs
- Correlation IDs: request_id + marketplace_purchase_token/entitlement_id where available
- Emit a single “reconcile summary” log per subscription: changed=true/false, writes=N, elapsed_ms, error_class

E) Runbook + on-call ergonomics
- Add runbook section to internal docs:
  - How to identify partner-side propagation delays vs Redwood failures
  - How to manually re-trigger sync safely
  - How to inspect subscription state, entitlement rows, and outbox backlog
  - Common error codes and mitigation

Acceptance criteria
- Scheduled job is idempotent: re-running produces no additional side effects when partner snapshot is unchanged.
- On transient upstream failures, job retries and succeeds within the same run when possible.
- Alerting triggers before customer escalation in common failure modes:
  - partner API 5xx burst
  - credential/config errors
  - DB constraint / deadlock issues
- Support can follow runbook to determine if issue is (a) entitlement not yet propagated at partner, (b) sync failing, or (c) org provisioning failing.
- Measured improvement: median subscription→org entitlement availability reduced (target: < 5 minutes for the “healthy path”).

Implementation notes
- Prefer outbox pattern for provisioning side effects to avoid “fetch ok + write partial + notify twice” scenarios.
- Ensure uniqueness constraints are compatible with existing data; add a backfill/migration plan if needed.
- Retry policy should not amplify partner throttling; respect 429 Retry-After if provided.

title: Oscillating prefetch orchestrator for mixed-length continuous scheduler tuning
preview:
Problem: Mixed prompt/response workloads (web chat + API summarization) cause the continuous-batcher to oscillate between aggressive prefetching and conservative slot allocation. That oscillation increases GPU memory churn and KV cache fragmentation, resulting in 10-25% tail latency spikes and up to 12% higher memory pressure on p4d nodes in production.

Goal: Reduce oscillation and stabilize KV residency while preserving user-facing latency SLOs for short-turn interactive flows and long-turn generation. Implement a scheduler-level damping mechanism ("oscillation damping") and a KV bank rebalancer that proactively coils prefetched KV into compact banks to reduce fragmentation during bursts. Validate on stage with synthetic mixed-length traces and a small customer synthetic traffic test.

Context: We've tuned length-aware batching and prefetch windows independently, but the coupling between slot admission, prefetch aggressiveness, and KV compaction hasn't been addressed holistically. Recent postmortems (ENG-472888) show repeated GC-like KV evictions during microbursts of long responses interleaved with many short prompts. This ticket explores a runtime-level orchestrator to smooth prefetch decisions across time windows and GPU memory banks.

title: Marketplace metering dimension mapping (AWS/GCP) aligned to SKU catalog v2
preview:
## Context
Marketplace refresh introduces new SKUs (Hosted / Dedicated / Private + add-ons). Engineering needs to ensure that:
1) Marketplace billing dimensions (AWS + GCP) match the SKU catalog v2 naming/IDs
2) The internal billing pipeline can unambiguously map marketplace usage records to Redwood meters and invoice line items
3) Entitlements and limits derived from the subscription resolve to the correct plan/SKU in Console onboarding

We have existing legacy marketplace dimensions and internal meters that do not align 1:1 with the new catalog, and we already saw a mismatch in the wild (ref: SUP-4829 + Slack #eng thread “billing dimension mismatch found”). This is a launch blocker because it affects customer trust, invoice reconciliation, and marketplace review.

## Problem statement
Today, marketplace usage events and invoice lines can be inconsistent due to:
- Dimension name drift across systems (partner portal config vs internal billing catalog)
- Multiple “token” concepts (prompt/completion/total) being aggregated differently per integration
- Dedicated/Private constructs being represented as usage even when they should be contract dimensions (or vice versa)
- Missing/incorrect SKU identifiers in our billing catalog, causing fallback mapping and incorrect line descriptions

## Goals
- Ship a canonical mapping for all marketplace SKUs v2 to internal billing meters.
- Validate mapping end-to-end in staging (subscribe → entitlement → usage → metering record → invoice preview).
- Add guardrails so we fail closed (explicit errors + alerting) when a dimension is unknown, rather than silently mis-billing.

## Non-goals
- Redesign of pricing (rates, commitments, discounting). This ticket only ensures technical alignment of identifiers and dimensions.
- Backfill of historical invoices beyond a limited reconciliation window (handled with Finance/RevRec separately).

## Proposed approach
### 1) Define canonical identifiers
- Source of truth: internal billing catalog ("billing-catalog" service config).
- Each marketplace SKU must have:
  - `marketplace_sku_id` (Redwood canonical ID; used in entitlement + billing)
  - AWS dimension key(s) where applicable
  - GCP billing dimension(s) / metric mapping
  - Internal meter key(s)

### 2) Normalize “Hosted” usage dimensions
- Marketplace should meter on total tokens for Hosted API (not separate prompt vs completion) for simplicity, consistent with SKU catalog v2.
- Internal pipeline keeps prompt/completion for analytics, but marketplace reporting aggregates to total tokens at the integration boundary.
- Add an integration test that asserts Hosted token aggregation is deterministic and consistent with the API usage ledger.

### 3) Dedicated and Private
- Dedicated: usage should map to contract dimensions as defined by the marketplace offer structure (capacity reservation / throughput tier), and not double-count tokens.
  - We still record tokens internally for observability/cost dashboards but must not report token usage to marketplace unless explicitly configured.
- Private: platform fee/license is contract-only; any optional add-ons that are usage-based must have separate explicit dimensions.

### 4) Safety checks + alerting
- Unknown dimension key from AWS/GCP ingestion → reject usage record, emit `metering.dimension_unknown` alert, and tag subscription + org for follow-up.
- Add a daily reconciliation job/report (counts by dimension) to catch drift after partner portal edits.

## Implementation tasks
- [x] Update internal billing catalog with marketplace SKU IDs and dimension mapping
- [x] Add mapping layer in metering ingestion that:
  - validates dimension keys
  - maps to internal meter keys
  - aggregates Hosted tokens correctly
- [x] Add automated tests:
  - unit tests for mapping table completeness
  - integration test in staging: synthetic subscription + usage → expected marketplace record
- [x] Add dashboards/alerts for unknown dimensions and mapping failures
- [x] Coordinate with Partnerships to verify partner portal dimension keys match exactly (case-sensitive)

## Acceptance criteria
1) For each SKU in SKU catalog v2, there is a single canonical mapping to:
   - AWS dimension key(s)
   - GCP dimension key(s)
   - internal meter(s)
2) In staging, we can run an end-to-end scenario for:
   - Hosted usage-based subscription: 3 requests produce marketplace usage records with expected dimension + total tokens
   - Dedicated subscription: no Hosted token usage is emitted to marketplace for the org
3) Unknown dimensions fail closed and page/alert (not silently mapped).
4) Finance can reconcile marketplace line items against Redwood usage ledger for a defined test period with <1% variance (expected from rounding), documented in runbook.

## Risks / gotchas
- AWS/GCP dimension keys are case-sensitive and changes may require re-approval; we must avoid last-minute renames.
- Entitlement propagation delays can look like “missing SKU mapping”; we need clear logging to distinguish.
- Multi-subscription orgs: ensure metering routes to the correct subscription/entitlement (handled primarily in ENG-22115, but mapping must not assume 1:1).

## Rollout plan
- Phase 1: ship mapping + fail-closed validation in staging, run synthetic test suite.
- Phase 2: enable in production for new marketplace SKUs only (feature flag `marketplace_sku_v2_mapping`).
- Phase 3: migrate remaining legacy SKUs (if any) or keep legacy mapping table until listings are fully deprecated.

## Links / references
- Confluence: marketplace SKU catalog v2 (pricing-and-packaging)
- Confluence: AWS marketplace offer structure
- Confluence: GCP marketplace SKU + billing dimensions
- GitHub PR: redwood/pr-19874-add-marketplace-sku-ids-to-billing-catalog.json
- Support: SUP-4829-marketplace-billing-dimension-mismatch
- Slack: #eng "billing dimension mismatch found" (1769621188)

## Notes for Support/RevOps
If a customer reports unexpected marketplace line items:
1) Check org subscription entitlements in Console → Billing → Marketplace
2) Verify dimension keys emitted in `metering-ingest` logs
3) If `dimension_unknown` present, open incident + coordinate with Partnerships for partner portal drift

title: Marketplace entitlement verification API (AWS/GCP) for subscription → org provisioning flow
preview:
## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

text:
## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

### Telemetry hooks
Coordinate event names w/ Nadia Rahman (ENG-22114):
- `mp_verify_start`, `mp_verify_result` (status + error_code)
- `mp_link_result`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POST `:verify` returns deterministic status (`entitled`/`pending`/`not_entitled`/`blocked`) for AWS and GCP test subscriptions.
- [ ] GET entitlement endpoint returns last-known record including `last_synced_at`.
- [ ] POST `:link` is idempotent and enforces org-admin permission.
- [ ] Error codes above are implemented and documented in internal API docs.
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover:
  - pending propagation
  - inactive/expired
  - already-linked
  - multi-subscription per org
  - stale sync record triggering refresh enqueue
- [ ] Audit logs/events are emitted and visible in staging.

## Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag `marketplace_entitlement_verification_v2` (default off).
2) Enable in staging; run end-to-end test plan from Partnerships (AWS/GCP).
3) Enable for internal dogfood orgs + a small % of marketplace onboarding traffic.
4) Full rollout after 48h with no increase in provisioning-delay tickets.

## Notes from partner + support feedback

- We must avoid wording that implies Redwood can see full marketplace billing details; keep responses minimal.
