title: Segment pruning + materialized views to accelerate tenant dashboard reads
preview:
Background: Interactive tenant dashboards (Usage Overview, Top Models, Live Tokens) currently suffer from high p95 query latencies during peak windows. Queries that scan timeseries records for the last 30/90/365 days frequently hit deep partitions and large indices, producing median p95s of ~850ms and spikes >2.5s for multi-tenant orgs. The usage analytics pipeline emits fine-grained events (per-request tokens, model_id, tenant_id, region) which are stored in the analytics store and aggregated at read time for the console dashboards.

Problem statement: Many dashboard queries are read-heavy, low-cardinality (top-K, timeseries rolls) and can be answered from pre-aggregated state. Current query plan often does full index range scans for scenes where we could apply segment (time) pruning and use compact MV (daily/hourly) to avoid scanning hot partitions. Deep offset pagination exacerbates the cost for long lists (e.g., tenant with many models).

Goal: Reduce dashboard query p95 to <350ms for 95% of tenants and 95th percentile result set size requests. Reduce backend CPU and RCU consumption for Console read paths by 40% for peak periods by shipping segment pruning + materialized views + seek-based pagination.

Proposed approach (three complementary tracks):
1) Segment pruning and index reordering: introduce explicit time-segmented index mapping in the analytics store (partition keys ordered as tenant_id|date_bucket|model_id). Implement planner-level segment pruning so queries with time bounds only touch relevant date buckets.
2) Materialized views (MVs) for common dashboard panels: create MV pipelines that compute daily and hourly aggregates per tenant for metrics used by dashboards (tokens_by_model, requests_per_minute, avg_latency_by_model). Persist MVs in a compact table with TTLs and near-real-time updates using the streaming aggregation job (Kafka->Flink->MV sink). Provide read-only API endpoints that prefer MVs when the query window falls within MV coverage.
3) Seek-pagination + prefetch cache: replace deep OFFSET pagination for the model list with seek-based cursor pagination (composite key: last_seen_score|model_id) to avoid scanning. Add a per-tenant Redis prefetch cache for first-page results and top-N frequently requested time windows. Cache invalidation via MV update hooks (pub/sub).

Trade-offs and notes:
- Materialized views increase storage and pipeline complexity; we mitigate by limiting MVs to high-traffic tenants and panels (adaptive MV creation).
- Near-real-time requirement (<1m freshness for last-24h) means MV streaming updates rather than batch-only. That increases pipeline resource use; we'll autoscale the aggregation job for high-throughput tenants.
- Segment pruning requires careful migration of existing indices; we will backfill segment mappings in a rolling fashion and keep compatibility shim for old index format for 2 weeks.

Implementation plan (milestones):
A) Design + schema changes (1 week): index mapping, MV schema, API hints. Link to design doc in attachments.
B) MV streaming job prototype (2 weeks): Flink job producing hourly MV for one panel (tokens_by_model) for a pilot tenant.
C) Query planner: implement time-bucket pruning and planner hinting in analytics read-service (2 weeks).
D) Pagination + cache: swap client/console route to cursor pagination and add Redis prefetch (1 week).
E) Pilot and rollout (2 weeks): pilot on 3 medium customers, collect metrics, ramp to all.

Success criteria / Acceptance tests:
- P95 endpoint latency for dashboard panels falls below 350ms for pilot tenants under peak load (measured during synthetic replay).
- Backend CPU and RCU usage for console read-service reduced by >=40% at 95th percentile for pilot tenants.
- Cache hit rate for first-page reads >=75% after warmup.
- No stale data incidents in pilot (MV freshness within 60s 99% of the time).

Rollout plan and feature flags:
- Feature flag gated per-tenant: mv_enabled, segment_prune_enabled, cursor_pagination_enabled. Start with mv_enabled for pilot tenants only.
- Gradual ramp: pilot -> 10% orgs -> 50% -> all. Monitor errors and fallback to direct query if MV not available.

Monitoring and dashboards:
- Console dashboard "ENG/QueryPerf/SegmentMV" (link in attachments) will show p50/p95 latencies, CPU, RCU, MV freshness, cache hit rates, and fallback counts to direct queries.
- Alerting: page on p95 > 1s for >5m or MV freshness > 120s for >10% of updates.

Backward compatibility and rollback:
- Read-service will detect MV unavailability and automatically fall back to original query plan. Migration will include a compatibility layer that accepts both old and new index shapes. If errors >0.5% or latencies regress, automated rollback disabling MV and segment pruning for affected tenants.

References and links are in attachments.

Note: This ticket focuses on the server-side query and pipeline pieces. Console UI changes to prefer cursor pagination are tracked as a separate design ticket in product-management.

title: Quant QA harness: golden-corpus orchestration, stratified sampling, and semantic gating
preview:
Goal: Build an automated Quantization QA harness that validates FP8/INT8 calibration profiles against a verifiable golden corpus and produces deterministic pass/fail gates suitable for CI, canary rollouts, and Dedicated/Private deployments. This ticket focuses on the orchestration layer and validation logic: golden corpus selection and curation, stratified sampling, semantic + token-level metrics, composite gating rules, artifacts storage, and CI integration.

Why: Current quantization validation runs produce ad-hoc reports and manual signoffs. We need reproducible, auditable, and explainable gates so model owners can safely accept quant profiles into runtime and autoscale pools without regressing customer-facing quality.

Scope:
- Define golden-corpus curation process and metadata schema (source, intent, distribution buckets, provenance).
- Implement stratified sampler that ensures coverage across sequence lengths, perplexity bands, instruction complexity, and domain buckets (search, chat, summarization, code).
- Implement metric set: embedding cosine similarity (avg and tail), token-level edit-distance ratio, LM perplexity delta, n-gram overlap, and KL divergence on next-token distribution for targeted positions.
- Build composite gate engine: per-profile thresholds, soft-fail flags, severity tags, and aggregated pass/fail decision with human-review hooks.
- Integrate harness into our CI pipeline (GitHub Actions) and our Console dashboard: store artifact bundles (reports, baseline outputs, diffs) to s3://redwood-quant-validation/<profile>/<timestamp>/ and expose links in the Linear ticket and release notes.
- Add replayability: deterministic seeds, canonical model checkpoints, and exact env specs to reproduce failed cases locally and on Dedicated/Private instances.
- Provide lightweight explainability: per-failure heatmap artifacts, most-impacted prompts, and suggested calibration adjustments (quant scale tweaks, block-wise FP8 overrides).

Out of scope for this ticket: building the quantization kernels themselves (handled by runtime team), full UI work for Console (we wire basic links and a minimal report viewer).

title: Chronicle Atlas: Micro-journey Mapping & Cost Lensing Prototype
preview: Goal: Prototype a compact, context-aware navigation overlay (Chronicle Atlas) that surfaces micro-journeys across Console surfaces (Dashboards, Rollouts, Cost Views) and folds in a lightweight cost-lensing control. The Atlas is intended to reduce cognitive load when switching contexts, surface relevant rollouts and cost metrics inline, and provide predictable routing behavior for multi-workspace customers. Scope: IA sketch -> mid-fidelity prototype -> internal usability session -> iterate on placement/interaction. Out of scope for this ticket: backend telemetry plumbing, RBAC enforcement, and Dedicated deployment config view changes (these are follow-ups tracked under ENG tickets).

title: Adaptive-window token valuation & microbilling bridge for streaming requests
preview: Problem: Our current billing pipeline attributes token costs using ingestion-time windows and naive dedupe that breaks for long-lived streaming sessions and multi-part chunked responses. Late-arriving events (network retries, out-of-order shards) cause either double-billing or missing charges, and tenants with high variability (low-latency mobile clients) see noisy spikes. Goal: Build a streaming-aware microbilling bridge that performs event-time aligned token valuation with adaptive watermarking, deterministic request fingerprints for idempotent dedupe, and a tenant-configurable grace window. The bridge should operate as a lightweight pre-aggregation layer between the streaming ingestion gateway and the existing ledger reconciler so we can deploy it incrementally without changing downstream billing semantics.

title: Geo provisioning orchestration spec and sales handback guide
preview:
Objective: Define a pragmatic orchestration specification and a repeatable sales handback guide for enterprise region/data-residency requests (new region provisioning, failover enablement, and strict residency constraints). This doc ties eligibility checks, cost estimation, provisioning runway, expected lead times, and a clear handoff checklist for Sales/SEs into a single pipeline that can be operationalized by PM, Solutions, and Infra teams.

Context: Customers increasingly ask for region-specific availability for compliance, latency, and contractual residency. Current intake is ad-hoc: Sales submits email threads, Infra does ad-hoc sizing, and PMs coordinate case-by-case. That creates long lead times and inconsistent contractual language. We need a spec that: 1) standardizes decision gates (eligibility, risk, capacity), 2) produces a fast, defensible cost and timeline estimate, and 3) provides Sales with a one-page handback and clear escalation triggers.

Scope: New public region onboard requests and enterprise-only private residency (VPC/on-prem) requests tied to contractual SLAs. Excludes routine failover testing for existing region pairs (that will be covered by a separate runbook).

title: SLO-horizon interruptor with KV tenancy decay and progressive preemption
preview: Problem: Continuous batching improves GPU utilization but introduces complex preemption trade-offs when GPU memory (KV cache + model state) becomes constrained. Current hard-preempt approaches either violate tenant latency SLOs or evict cache too aggressively, causing quality regressions and thundering re-warm. Goal: design and implement an SLO-aware interruptor that (1) minimizes SLO breaches, (2) reduces harmful KV thrash, and (3) reclaims memory in a graded, predictable way.

title: API Token Retirement, Escrow & Scope Assertion Playbook
preview:
Problem: Current token lifecycle is inconsistent across hosted/Dedicated/Private. We lack a single playbook for: emergency revocation, escrowed rotation, deterministic naming, and cryptographic scope assertions that tie tokens to RBAC v2 principals. This causes delayed responses to incidents and makes forensic audit trails noisy.

Goal: Produce an operational playbook and implement infra pieces to enable: (1) automated rotation with escrow and phased retirement, (2) per-scope token issuance (least privilege by default), (3) deterministic, searchable naming conventions, (4) instant cross-region revocation propagation, and (5) explicit audit links between token events and SSO/SAML/Identity assertions.

Scope: Hosted API tokens, service-account tokens, per-scope scoped keys (read/write/embed), admin tokens used by Console and internal services. Not in scope: customer-managed HSM key material lifecycle (handled in separate customer-onprem project).

We lack a single playbook for: emergency revocation, escrowed rotation, deterministic naming, and cryptographic scope assertions that tie tokens to RBAC v2 principals. This causes delayed responses to incidents and makes forensic audit trails noisy.

Goal: Produce an operational playbook and implement infra pieces to enable: (1) automated rotation with escrow and phased retirement, (2) per-scope token issuance (least privilege by default), (3) deterministic, searchable naming conventions, (4) instant cross-region revocation propagation, and (5) explicit audit links between token events and SSO/SAML/Identity assertions.

Scope: Hosted API tokens, service-account tokens, per-scope scoped keys (read/write/embed), admin tokens used by Console and internal services. Not in scope: customer-managed HSM key material lifecycle (handled in separate customer-onprem project).

title: Tenant-aware Tempo ingest adapter with ordered ACKs and dead-letter handling
preview: Build a Tempo-compatible ingest adapter that sits in front of our Tempo/OTLP export path to handle legacy batched span envelopes (Zipkin/Jaeger HTTP batches, older OTLP variants). The adapter must: (1) accept multiple legacy formats and normalize to OTLP v1; (2) perform per-tenant rate adaptation and backpressure to prevent noisy tenants from impacting others; (3) provide ordered ACK semantics for fanout scenarios so that downstream replays/dual-writes maintain causal ordering where required; (4) surface malformed or unverifiable batches to a dead-letter store with metadata for debugging; and (5) expose tracing and SLO metrics for ingestion latency, queue depth, dropped/bounced batches, and dead-letter rates. This component will be deployed as a regional, horizontally-scalable service alongside the existing OTLP ingress gateway and will be the primary compatibility shim during a phased migration of legacy clients.

title: Control plane: extend route config with quantization_profile_ref (default-safe) + guardrails
preview:
## Context
We are adding an opt-in Quantization Profile Library. For v1, enablement is route-level (not tenant-wide default), and default behavior must be unchanged.

This ticket extends the control-plane route configuration schema to include a quantization profile reference (`quantization_profile_ref`) that can be:
- `default` (or unset): preserve baseline precision behavior
- an explicit profile ref: apply a specific published profile version

This work also includes guardrails and migration notes so that existing routes remain valid, and incompatible/unknown values fail safely.

## Goals
1) Add a route-level config field for quantization enablement that is backwards compatible.
2) Ensure safe behavior when the field is missing/invalid/incompatible (do not break serving; fall back to baseline).
3) Provide clear API semantics + error messaging for Console and SDK consumers.
4) Land a migration plan that avoids thundering herds / config cache churn.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Global tenant defaults (separate discussion; can be layered later)
- Aliases like `recommended`/`latest` that can silently change without explicit customer action
- Per-route automatic profile selection

## Proposed config shape
### Route config (conceptual)
- `quantization_profile_ref`: string (optional)
  - Allowed values:
    - omitted / null => treated as `default`
    - `default` => baseline mode
    - `profile:<profile_id>@<version>` => explicit pinned profile version

Notes:
- We intentionally require an explicit version for GA stability. Any alias semantics should be added later with strong change-control semantics.
- Validation should occur on write (config API) and on read/resolve (policy evaluation), with safe fallback.

## API behavior
### Write-time validation (Config API)
- If `quantization_profile_ref` is absent/null/"default": accept.
- If it is present:
  - Validate format: must be either `default` or `profile:<id>@<version>`.
  - Validate that `<id>@<version>` exists in registry *at time of write* (best-effort; allow eventual consistency but prefer early feedback).
  - If registry is temporarily unavailable: return a retriable error (HTTP 503) for explicit profile refs; do not allow writing unknown refs.

### Resolve-time semantics (Policy engine / router)
- If field is absent/null/"default": resolved mode is baseline.
- If explicit profile:
  - Policy engine performs deterministic compatibility checks (model variant, GPU SKU class, max context constraints). If incompatible, resolved mode becomes baseline and emits a structured reason.

### Error / warning taxonomy (for Console)
- Hard errors (block enablement): invalid format; unknown profile id/version; RBAC denial.
- Soft guardrails (allow save but warns / will fallback): compatible-at-write but not guaranteed at runtime due to region/hardware constraints; runtime will fallback.

## Guardrails
- Backwards compatibility: old route payloads without the new field must deserialize.
- Safety: any malformed/unknown config should never cause request failures; it should either block saving or resolve to baseline.
- Determinism: resolution outcome must be stable for a given (route config + model + hardware class + request context) and must be emitted in logs/metrics downstream (runtime work tracked elsewhere).

## Migration notes
We need to add a nullable column/field in route config persistence and ensure:
- default is logically baseline without writing anything (avoid large-scale rewrites)
- serializers/deserializers tolerate the field in both directions
- staged rollout:
  1) ship readers (able to parse new field) everywhere
  2) ship writers (Console/API) behind feature flag/allowlist
  3) enable field write for beta tenants only

Implementation should coordinate with:
- ENG-5815 (policy engine compatibility checks)
- ENG-5813 (profile registry storage + read APIs)
- ENG-5819 (global kill switch)

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Route config schema includes `quantization_profile_ref` (optional) and supports round-trip serialization.
- [x] Existing routes continue to function with no config changes.
- [x] API rejects invalid refs with actionable error message.
- [x] Explicit refs are pinned to a specific version (no implicit alias semantics).
- [x] Migration is deployed without rewriting existing routes.
- [x] Docs in code comments include examples and behavior details.

## Testing
- Unit tests: parsing/validation; default behavior; invalid values; unknown profiles.
- Integration tests (lightweight): write route config with explicit ref; read back; confirm policy resolution returns either applied or fallback state.

## Implementation links
- Primary PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-18411-route-config-add-quantization-profile-ref.json
- Follow-up migration/back-compat PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-18605-add-config-migration-for-route-quantization-field.json

## Open questions
1) Should we allow a shorter ref format (`<id>@<version>`) vs prefixing `profile:`?
   - Current proposal uses `profile:` prefix to keep room for future ref types.
2) Should write-time validation require registry existence, or accept and rely on resolve-time fallback?
   - Current proposal: require existence for explicit refs to avoid silent no-ops.

title: Policy engine support for stage gates (deterministic eval, caching, failure semantics)
preview:
## Context
Dedicated staged rollouts introduce explicit rollout stages (e.g., 1%/10%/50%/100% traffic or replica partitions) for Dedicated pools. Promotion between stages must be gated on objective signals (error rate, p95/p99 latency, saturation/headroom, GPU health, OOM, drops/429, etc.).

This ticket adds *first-class gate evaluation* to the policy engine so that:
- Gate results are deterministic and auditable.
- The system behaves predictably under telemetry lag/outages (failure handling semantics are explicit).
- Evaluation is cheap enough to run continuously without impacting request routing latency.

Non-goal: building a general experimentation system. Gates are used strictly to decide whether a rollout stage is eligible for promotion / should auto-abort.

## Requirements
### Functional
1) Implement gate evaluation for DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan stages in the policy engine.
   - Inputs: (account_id, pool_id, rollout_plan_id, stage_id, stage_start_ts, eval_time) + gate definitions (signal + threshold + window + min samples) + rollout tier.
   - Outputs: per-gate result (PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN) and overall decision.

2) Provide deterministic evaluation:
   - Given identical inputs (same eval_time rounded to bucket, same data availability), evaluator must produce identical results.
   - Eliminate nondeterminism from query ranges: all time windows are derived from eval_time with fixed rounding rules.
   - Explicit handling for missing data and partial data.

3) Failure handling semantics (per ADR + leadership direction):
   - Default behavior for telemetry staleness / missing required signals: **fail-closed** (gate => UNKNOWN/FAIL blocks promotion) with operator override (force-promote) gated by RBAC + audit.
   - Avoid flapping: require consecutive PASS evaluations (configurable) before promotion becomes available.
   - Define "UNKNOWN" consistently (e.g., missing series, insufficient samples, stale metrics) and propagate to UI.

4) Caching and performance:
   - Gate evaluation must not add noticeable latency to the routing hot path. Evaluation runs asynchronously and writes results to a store the Console / control plane can read.
   - Cache expensive query results with a bounded TTL and deterministic cache keys.
   - Provide rate limiting / backoff to avoid thundering herds when many rollouts evaluate at once.

5) Auditability:
   - Record evaluation inputs/outputs sufficient to reconstruct why a gate passed/failed (query template id, window, observed value, threshold, sample count, staleness flags).
   - Ensure evaluation events are attributable to a rollout_plan_id + stage_id and can be correlated with promote/abort audit events.

### Integration points
- Control plane rollout service triggers evaluation on schedule and on stage transitions (stage start / hold / resume).
- Telemetry pipeline must emit stage-tagged metrics (stage_id or stage_partition) so gates can evaluate the canary cohort (depends on ENG-5966).
- Console backend fetches gate evidence for display (ENG-5980), using the evaluator output schema.

## Proposed implementation (v1)
### 1) Gate model in policy engine
Add a GateEvaluator module:
- GateDefinition: {gate_type, signal, threshold, comparison, window, min_samples, aggregation, required, fail_behavior}
- GateResult: {status PASS|FAIL|UNKNOWN, observed_value, threshold, comparison, window_start, window_end, sample_count, staleness_seconds, reason_code, reason_detail, query_id}
- GateDecision: {overall_status, gates: [GateResult], computed_at}

Gate types supported in v1:
- Error rate (5xx + internal errors) over window
- p95 / p99 latency over window
- Saturation/headroom (GPU util + queue depth proxy)
- OOM rate / CUDA OOM counter
- Request drop / 429 rate
- GPU health (node taints/ECC/thermal throttle) (pass-through initially; see notes)

### 2) Determinism details
- eval_time is rounded to the nearest 60s boundary (configurable constant). All query windows align to that boundary.
- Use a fixed lookback window per gate (default 15m). For bursty workloads, min_samples enforces we don’t promote on noise.
- When telemetry arrives late, evaluator uses window_end = rounded(eval_time) and explicitly marks staleness based on ingest watermark.
- All floating point comparisons use a fixed epsilon and explicit rounding (e.g., observed_value rounded to 4 decimals for the decision record; raw value retained in trace logs).

### 3) Missing data and staleness
Define reason codes:
- INSUFFICIENT_SAMPLES: sample_count < min_samples
- SERIES_MISSING: required series absent for the window
- TELEMETRY_STALE: ingest watermark older than (window_end - staleness_budget)
- QUERY_ERROR: backend query failure/timeouts

Semantics:
- Required gates: UNKNOWN blocks promotion (treated as FAIL for overall decision)
- Optional gates: UNKNOWN is ignored for overall decision but shown in evidence

### 4) Caching
Two-tier caching:
- QueryResultCache keyed by (query_id, account_id, pool_id, stage_id, window_start, window_end)
  - TTL = 90s; max entries bounded; negative caching for SERIES_MISSING
- GateDecisionCache keyed by (rollout_plan_id, stage_id, window_end)
  - TTL = 60s; ensures Console refresh doesn’t re-query everything

### 5) Execution model
- Evaluations run in policy engine worker pool (NOT request thread).
- Triggered by:
  - periodic scheduler (every 60s per active rollout stage)
  - explicit trigger on stage start / resume
- Results written to GateDecisionStore with monotonic timestamps and idempotency:
  - primary key: (rollout_plan_id, stage_id, window_end)
  - write is upsert; retain last N decisions per stage for evidence history

### 6) Outputs / API contract (consumed by control plane + Console)
Provide an internal endpoint / method used by rollout service:
- EvaluateStageGates(rollout_plan_id, stage_id, eval_time) => GateDecision
- GetLatestStageGateDecision(rollout_plan_id, stage_id) => GateDecision

Schema must include:
- gate status, observed value, threshold, min_samples, window, time range
- staleness indicator + watermark age
- reason string suitable for UI (human readable but stable)

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Policy engine can compute gate decision for a stage using stage-tagged metrics.
- [x] Deterministic results: repeated evaluation in the same minute bucket returns identical decisions.
- [x] Missing metrics behave per spec (fail-closed for required gates) and are surfaced as UNKNOWN with reason codes.
- [x] Caching reduces duplicate queries (validated via metrics: cache hit rate > 70% during steady-state evaluations).
- [x] Evaluation cannot impact routing latency (p99 routing decision latency unchanged in canary).
- [x] Decision records are available to Console backend for evidence display.
- [x] Unit + integration tests cover: missing data, stale data, query timeout, flapping prevention.

## Testing plan
- Unit tests: window rounding, reason codes, threshold comparisons, min_samples.
- Golden tests: fixed synthetic timeseries -> expected PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN outputs.
- Integration tests in simulated Dedicated pool environment (paired with ENG-5995/PR integration):
  - Telemetry lag injection -> UNKNOWN -> promotion blocked
  - Error spike -> FAIL -> auto-abort trigger downstream
  - Metric backend timeout -> QUERY_ERROR -> promotion blocked

## Links
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-gate-evaluation-signals-and-thresholds.json
- Slack thread: missing metrics fail-open vs fail-closed: sources/slack/eng-platform/1738702219-missing-metrics-fail-open-vs-fail-closed.json
- Gate query drafts: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/gate-evaluation-query-drafts.json
- Dependent tickets: ENG-5932 (rollout plan APIs), ENG-5966 (stage-tagged telemetry)

## Notes / open items
- GPU health gates in v1: start with "node unhealthy percentage" aggregated per stage partition. More granular signals (ECC bursts, thermal throttle) may require separate pipelines and tighter freshness guarantees.
- Cardinality: stage_id is allowed, but tag allowlist must prevent exploding dimensions (account_id not allowed in global metrics; use internal partition keys).
- Promotion requires N consecutive PASS results (proposed N=2) to reduce flapping; confirm with SRE.

title: Precision-aware attention microkernel arbiter with speculative zero-copy demotion
preview:
Problem:
Serving runtime currently pays a measurable per-token cost on decode when switching between high-precision (FP16/FP32) and low-precision (INT8/FP8) attention kernels due to dequantization/ requantization, shadow KV copy semantics, and kernel setup overhead. These costs are most visible in workloads with mixed sequence length distributions and high prefix reuse (prefill-heavy chat workloads) where some heads or layers benefit from reduced precision but others do not.

Goal:
Design and implement an attention microkernel arbiter that chooses the optimal precision execution path at microkernel granularity, and that can speculatively apply a "zero-copy demotion" window to avoid immediate dequant/requant when short-lived precision switches are expected. The arbiter should: 1) minimize end-to-end latency regression for single-request decode paths, 2) reduce token-level cost by favoring lower-precision kernels when safe, 3) preserve KV cache correctness for prefill and streaming decode, and 4) emit telemetry to drive model/route-level heuristics in the console.

Proposed design (high level):
- Microkernel abstraction: Add a light-weight kernel descriptor that encodes precision, expected setup_cost_us, amortized_per_token_cost, and KV-copy-behavior (in-place | shadow-copy-required).
- Runtime arbitration policy: For each attention invocation we run a deterministic selection function that combines: (a) remaining query length budget (decode path latency SLO), (b) historical per-model head/ layer precision error profile, (c) kernel setup amortization window, and (d) current KV ownership flags.
- Speculative zero-copy demotion window: When a high-precision -> low-precision transition is predicted but the window of low-precision usage is small (e.g., < N tokens or < T ms), we speculatively execute the low-precision kernel using an on-the-fly view that avoids duplicating the KV buffer. If numerical invariants fail (tracked via cheap checksum or sampled validation), we fall back and pay the dequantation penalty once and correct state.
- Mixed-precision per-head admission: Allow per-head selection for transformer architectures that support headwise scaling. The selection is constrained to kernels that can accept mixed-head inputs without additional copies; otherwise, fall back to layerwise execution.
- Telemetry & sampling: Add events for selection decisions, fallback reasons, measured setup cost, and checksum validation failures. Provide a sampling config to limit validation checks to ~0.5-2% of tokens in production.

Implementation tasks (epic):
- Kernel descriptor and API additions to runtime (C++): descriptors, registration, and perf metadata.
- Arbiter logic in serving pipeline (C++/Rust): selection function implementation, budget accounting, KV ownership checks.
- Zero-copy demotion helper: lightweight view semantics, validation checksum, and safe fallback path.
- Per-head admission prototype for attention kernels: integrate with per-head sharding code paths.
- Telemetry hooks: events, metrics, and trace annotations.
- Benchmarks: microbench for setup cost, end-to-end decode on representative models (7B/13B/70B), tail-latency p99/p995 tracking, and cost-per-token estimation.
- Canary plan and rollout: start with non-critical internal route, enable sampling-only telemetry, then enable demotion with validation, finally enable fully optimistic mode after 2 weeks of stable metrics.

Risks and mitigations:
- Silent numerical regressions: mitigate with sampled validation checks, automated quality-regression alerts tied to prompt-evals.
- KV state corruption on optimistic path: isolate with strong invariants and immediate corrective fallback plus post-mortem tracing.
- Increased code complexity: keep microkernel descriptor small and well-documented; add unit tests and fuzz tests for boundary cases.

Acceptance criteria:
- Arbiter selects reduced-precision kernels for at least 25% of eligible attention invocations on target workloads without increasing p99 latency by >5% on decode-heavy benchmarks.
- Token-level cost reduction measured >= 8% on mixed-length chat workload compared to baseline runtime-1.18 under same throughput.
- No silent numerical regressions observed in sampled validation over 1M tokens (0/1M failure tolerance), and generated metrics surface root-cause for any fallback.
- End-to-end CI includes kernel-level unit tests, integration tests for KV correctness, and perf regression tests.

Notes/constraints:
- This feature must be compatible with existing on-prem/private deployments but can be gated by a config flag.
- For Dedicated customers with strict determinism SLAs we default to conservative (no speculative demotion) unless explicitly enabled.

Design and implement an attention microkernel arbiter that chooses the optimal precision execution path at microkernel granularity, and that can speculatively apply a "zero-copy demotion" window to avoid immediate dequant/requant when short-lived precision switches are expected. title: Screenshot style matrix and redaction strategies for onboarding assets
preview:
Goal: Create a single, prescriptive style matrix and automated redaction strategy for onboarding/quickstart screenshots and doc visuals that guarantees brand consistency, protects PII, and is safe across locales and platform sizes. Deliver a matrix of canonical compositions (compact, regular, extended), locale-aware crop rules, rule-based redaction masks, and an observable QA checklist so PM/Docs/Support can reliably produce release-ready assets without manual rework.

Scope:
- Define canonical screenshot compositions for three densities and two aspect ratios (desktop, tablet).
- Redaction strategies: token replacement (faux data), patterned blur, and edge-preserving mask overlays; map each rule to sensitivity level (public, internal, sensitive).
- Localization safety: anchoring rules for LTR/RTL languages; overflow handling for long translations; typographic fallbacks.
- Branding overlays: subtle watermark and color-safe UI corner treatments that survive cropping.
- Export presets and naming scheme for designers and engineers (Figma export tokens, .png/@2x, metadata).

Acceptance criteria:
1) Figma file contains a style matrix page with every composition + annotated crop guides.
2) Redaction palette and mask components are implemented as reusable variants in Figma.
3) A one-page Confluence guide documents when to use token replacement vs blur vs mask and includes visual examples.
4) Automation-ready export presets (naming + metadata) that match PRD and can be consumed by the release pipeline.
5) QA checklist with at least 10 test cases, including RTL screenshots and long-string truncation tests.

Design decisions and trade-offs:
- Prefer token replacement for user-facing names in marketing-style screenshots (preserves affordance) and patterned blur for log-level content that might leak structural cues.
- Chose soft-edge masks over heavy pixelation to preserve overall UI layout while protecting secrets; this reduces developer confusion when verifying flows but slightly increases visual complexity.
- Limited number of densities to keep asset explosion manageable; will add additional sizes per analytics-driven demand.

Next steps:
- Finalize mask component library and hand off to Engineering for export presets (ENG-3312).
- Run pilot with Docs team for console-2025.03 release assets and collect feedback.
- Add automation hooks (filename metadata and crop anchors) to the release checklist and create a lightweight Node script to validate exports.

title: Stable-channel image mirroring and artifact contract for CUDA/driver stacks
preview: Establish a stable-channel image mirroring workflow and a machine-readable artifact contract for CUDA and driver stacks to guarantee predictable upgrades across Dedicated and Private deployments. The aim is to enable a single trusted 'stable' registry mirror per region that contains signed images, SBOMs, and a compatibility contract describing which driver/CUDA/kernel ABI combinations are validated for each runtime image.

title: Cross-customer k-fold relevance harness & live NDCG/MRR signal plumbing
preview: Overview: Build a reusable k-fold evaluation harness and live telemetry plumbing to track NDCG and MRR per-customer and per-promptset, enable stratified sampling for offline evals, and add lightweight A/B hooks on the reranking endpoint for experiment gating. This ticket covers the eval harness, schema changes for signal ingestion, a reference Jupyter/Notion notebook for analysts, and a plan for a gradual rollout with canary A/B tests. Goals: 1) Produce consistent offline NDCG/MRR scores across customer prompt sets and model variants; 2) Surface live NDCG/MRR baselines in the Console and tie regression alerts to prompt sets; 3) Ensure reranker endpoint changes do not degrade P95 latency beyond 10ms and scale to 500 QPS with batching; 4) Provide simple experiment flags for product teams to run canary comparisons with minimal infra changes.

title: SDK artifact attestation and cross-registry publish flow (PyPI / npm / Go modules)
preview: Implement an end-to-end artifact attestation and publish flow for Redwood SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go). Goals: 1) produce deterministic, provenance-bearing artifacts (wheels/tarballs, npm packages, go modules), 2) sign and attest artifacts using Sigstore + in-toto where possible, 3) extend CI to perform reproducible builds and cross-registry publishes, 4) add lightweight runtime verification helpers in each SDK so enterprise customers can validate signatures locally. This ticket covers CI pipelines, release matrix changes, registry credentials handling, and adding examples demonstrating verification. Coordination needed with SDK maintainers to ensure packaging changes do not alter runtime behavior (esp. streaming ergonomics and retry wrappers).

title: Self-service model intake: signed-weight manifest, conversion sandbox, and deterministic smoke suite
preview: Build a self-service, end-to-end intake pipeline for community/OSS model weights that enforces signed manifests, performs reproducible conversion in an isolated sandbox, and runs a deterministic smoke test + lightweight perf profiling to decide an initial pin recommendation. This ticket covers the gateway contract and the first-phase runner (conversion + smoke) for MLP/Transformer artifacts. The goal is to reduce manual triage, shorten time-to-publish, and eliminate common conversion failure modes that block downstream runtime onboarding.

title: Demand heatmap + feasibility snapshot for Sales/CS enterprise asks
preview: Create a compact, repeatable intake artifact that Sales/CS can use to consolidate enterprise feature requests into a demand heatmap and a one-page feasibility snapshot. The goal is to reduce lead time from first-sales-ask to an actionable PM/Eng scoping decision from weeks to days. This ticket delivers: 1) an intake template for frontline reps that balances signal capture with low friction, 2) an automated scoring rubric (ARR, #accounts, strategic fit, legal/regulatory risk, infra/runtime compatibility), 3) a template "feasibility snapshot" that includes rough effort band, risks, suggested model/runtime path (hosted / dedicated / private), and 4) a simple dashboard CSV schema for aggregation and heatmap generation.

title: Isolated VPC: Minimal Network Binding, Offline Upgrade Safety Contract, and Air‑gapped Acceptance Tests
preview: Purpose: Define a minimally permissive networking and offline-upgrade acceptance contract for Private/VPC deployments that must be enforced by the operator and validated by Redwood before GA. This ticket captures the requirements, objective test cases, and operational runbooks needed to certify a customer environment as 'Offline‑Safe' (able to receive upgrades from an approved mirror) and 'Zero‑Egress Compliant' (no network connections to Redwood control plane required in normal operation). This PRD focuses on networking bindings, artifact sync/verification, upgrade choreography, observable checks, and failure-mode acceptance criteria.

title: Semantic roles & focus management sweep for components: buttons, tables, charts, tokens
preview: Goal: Complete a cross-component accessibility sweep focused on semantic roles, keyboard focus/order, skip/fallback behaviors, and contrast tokens for buttons, table controls, and data-visualization components so the design system is ready for the upcoming VPAT refresh and runtime consumers. This ticket covers design decisions, token adjustments, keyboard nav matrices, ARIA role recommendations, and a rollout plan with dev/QA acceptance criteria. Scope intentionally excludes content authoring flows (rich text editor) which are covered in another ticket.

title: Automated jurisdiction-aware access recertification and SCIM drift remediation
preview:
Goal: Build an automated workflow to run periodic access recertifications that are aware of tenant data residency constraints and to remediate SCIM provisioning drift for scoped service accounts. The feature should produce cryptographically verifiable evidence bundles suitable for audit exports and support per-tenant retention policies.

Background: Reducing manual work in enterprise audits remains high priority for enterprise customers operating across jurisdictions (EU, UK, CA, US). Current periodic access exports are manual and global; they do not filter by residency zone or automatically reconcile SCIM drift discovered during recertification. This ticket ties together three capabilities: (1) jurisdiction filters applied to access recertification runs, (2) automated SCIM reconciliation suggestions + optional self-heal for service accounts, (3) evidence bundling (signed manifests) with retention metadata for export into compliance pipelines.

Success criteria: 
- Admins can schedule recertification runs scoped to one or more residency zones (e.g., eu-west, us-east).
- Recertification identifies entitlements changed since last run, flags high-risk roles, and surfaces suggested remediations.
- SCIM reconciliation can detect attribute drift and either create a PR-style remediation or perform auto-fix for service accounts where policy allows.
- System produces a signed evidence bundle (JSON + signature + hash) for each run and stores a copy per tenant respecting retention settings.
- End-to-end test coverage and a demo flow for Solutions Engineers.

Non-goals: This ticket does not implement tenant UI for configuring retention windows (separate PM ticket), nor does it change core RBAC model semantics.
